Our comprehensive documentation at claude.ai/code_ _covers all the features mentioned in this post and provides additional examples, implementation details, and advanced techniques._<br/><br/>## 1\. Customize your setup<br/><br/>Claude Code is an agentic coding assistant that automatically pulls context into prompts. This context gathering consumes time and tokens, but you can optimize it through environment tuning.<br/><br/>### a. Create `CLAUDE.md` files<br/><br/>`CLAUDE.md` is a special file that Claude automatically pulls into context when starting a conversation. This makes it an ideal place for documenting:<br/><br/>* Common bash commands<br/>* Core files and utility functions<br/>* Code style guidelines<br/>* Testing instructions<br/>* Repository etiquette (e.g., branch naming, merge vs. rebase, etc.)<br/>* Developer environment setup (e.g., pyenv use, which compilers work)<br/>* Any unexpected behaviors or warnings particular to the project<br/>* Other information you want Claude to remember<br/><br/>There’s no required format for `CLAUDE.md` files. We recommend keeping them concise and human-readable. For example:<br/><br/>```<br/># Bash commands<br/>- npm run build: Build the project<br/>- npm run typecheck: Run the typechecker<br/><br/># Code style<br/>- Use ES modules (import/export) syntax, not CommonJS (require)<br/>- Destructure imports when possible (eg. import \{ foo \} from 'bar')<br/><br/># Workflow<br/>- Be sure to typecheck when you’re done making a series of code changes<br/>- Prefer running single tests, and not the whole test suite, for performance<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>You can place `CLAUDE.md` files in several locations:<br/><br/>* **The root of your repo**, or wherever you run `claude` from (the most common usage). Name it `CLAUDE.md` and check it into git so that you can share it across sessions and with your team (recommended), or name it `CLAUDE.local.md` and `.gitignore` it<br/>* **Any parent of the directory** where you run `claude`. This is most useful for monorepos, where you might run `claude` from `root/foo`, and have `CLAUDE.md` files in both `root/CLAUDE.md` and `root/foo/CLAUDE.md`. Both of these will be pulled into context automatically<br/>* **Any child of the directory** where you run `claude`. This is the inverse of the above, and in this case, Claude will pull in `CLAUDE.md` files on demand when you work with files in child directories<br/>* **Your home folder** (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`), which applies it to all your _claude_ sessions<br/><br/>When you run the `/init` command, Claude will automatically generate a `CLAUDE.md` for you.<br/><br/>### b. Tune your `CLAUDE.md` files<br/><br/>Your `CLAUDE.md` files become part of Claude’s prompts, so they should be refined like any frequently used prompt. A common mistake is adding extensive content without iterating on its effectiveness. Take time to experiment and determine what produces the best instruction following from the model.<br/><br/>You can add content to your `CLAUDE.md` manually or press the `#` key to give Claude an instruction that it will automatically incorporate into the relevant `CLAUDE.md`. Many engineers use `#` frequently to document commands, files, and style guidelines while coding, then include `CLAUDE.md` changes in commits so team members benefit as well.<br/><br/>At Anthropic, we occasionally run `CLAUDE.md` files through the prompt improver and often tune instructions (e.g. adding emphasis with "IMPORTANT" or "YOU MUST") to improve adherence.<br/><br/>Claude Code tool allowlist<br/><br/>### c. Curate Claude's list of allowed tools<br/><br/>By default, Claude Code requests permission for any action that might modify your system: file writes, many bash commands, MCP tools, etc. We designed Claude Code with this deliberately conservative approach to prioritize safety. You can customize the allowlist to permit additional tools that you know are safe, or to allow potentially unsafe tools that are easy to undo (e.g., file editing, `git commit`).<br/><br/>There are four ways to manage allowed tools:<br/><br/>* **Select "Always allow"** when prompted during a session.<br/>* **Use the`/permissions`command** after starting Claude Code to add or remove tools from the allowlist. For example, you can add `Edit` to always allow file edits, `Bash(git commit:*)` to allow git commits, or `mcp__puppeteer__puppeteer_navigate` to allow navigating with the Puppeteer MCP server.<br/>* **Manually edit** your `.claude/settings.json` or `~/.claude.json` (we recommend checking the former into source control to share with your team)_._<br/>* **Use the` --allowedTools` CLI flag** for session-specific permissions.<br/><br/>### d. If using GitHub, install the gh CLI<br/><br/>Claude knows how to use the `gh` CLI to interact with GitHub for creating issues, opening pull requests, reading comments, and more. Without `gh` installed, Claude can still use the GitHub API or MCP server (if you have it installed).<br/><br/>## 2\. Give Claude more tools<br/><br/>Claude has access to your shell environment, where you can build up sets of convenience scripts and functions for it just like you would for yourself. It can also leverage more complex tools through MCP and REST APIs.<br/><br/>### a. Use Claude with bash tools<br/><br/>Claude Code inherits your bash environment, giving it access to all your tools. While Claude knows common utilities like unix tools and `gh`, it won't know about your custom bash tools without instructions:<br/><br/>1. Tell Claude the tool name with usage examples<br/>2. Tell Claude to run`--help`to see tool documentation<br/>3. Document frequently used tools in `CLAUDE.md`<br/><br/>### b. Use Claude with MCP<br/><br/>Claude Code functions as both an MCP server and client. As a client, it can connect to any number of MCP servers to access their tools in three ways:<br/><br/>* **In project config** (available when running Claude Code in that directory)<br/>* **In global config** (available in all projects)<br/>* **In a checked-in `.mcp.json` file** (available to anyone working in your codebase). For example, you can add Puppeteer and Sentry servers to your `.mcp.json`, so that every engineer working on your repo can use these out of the box.<br/><br/>When working with MCP, it can also be helpful to launch Claude with the `--mcp-debug` flag to help identify configuration issues.<br/><br/>### c. Use custom slash commands<br/><br/>For repeated workflows—debugging loops, log analysis, etc.—store prompt templates in Markdown files within the `.claude/commands` folder. These become available through the slash commands menu when you type `/`. You can check these commands into git to make them available for the rest of your team.<br/><br/>Custom slash commands can include the special keyword `$ARGUMENTS` to pass parameters from command invocation.<br/><br/>For example, here’s a slash command that you could use to automatically pull and fix a Github issue:<br/><br/>```<br/>Please analyze and fix the GitHub issue: $ARGUMENTS.<br/><br/>Follow these steps:<br/><br/>1. Use `gh issue view` to get the issue details<br/>2. Understand the problem described in the issue<br/>3. Search the codebase for relevant files<br/>4. Implement the necessary changes to fix the issue<br/>5. Write and run tests to verify the fix<br/>6. Ensure code passes linting and type checking<br/>7. Create a descriptive commit message<br/>8. Push and create a PR<br/><br/>Remember to use the GitHub CLI (`gh`) for all GitHub-related tasks.<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>Putting the above content into `.claude/commands/fix-github-issue.md` makes it available as the `/project:fix-github-issue` command in Claude Code. You could then for example use `/project:fix-github-issue 1234` to have Claude fix issue #1234\. Similarly, you can add your own personal commands to the`~/.claude/commands` folder for commands you want available in all of your sessions.<br/><br/>## 3\. Try common workflows<br/><br/>Claude Code doesn’t impose a specific workflow, giving you the flexibility to use it how you want. Within the space this flexibility affords, several successful patterns for effectively using Claude Code have emerged across our community of users:<br/><br/>### a. Explore, plan, code, commit<br/><br/>This versatile workflow suits many problems:<br/><br/>1. **Ask Claude to read relevant files, images, or URLs**, providing either general pointers ("read the file that handles logging") or specific filenames ("read logging.py"), but explicitly tell it not to write any code just yet.  <br/>   1. This is the part of the workflow where you should consider strong use of subagents, especially for complex problems. Telling Claude to use subagents to verify details or investigate particular questions it might have, especially early on in a conversation or task, tends to preserve context availability without much downside in terms of lost efficiency.<br/>2. **Ask Claude to make a plan for how to approach a specific problem**. We recommend using the word "think" to trigger extended thinking mode, which gives Claude additional computation time to evaluate alternatives more thoroughly. These specific phrases are mapped directly to increasing levels of thinking budget in the system: "think" < "think hard" < "think harder" < "ultrathink." Each level allocates progressively more thinking budget for Claude to use.  <br/>   1. If the results of this step seem reasonable, you can have Claude create a document or a GitHub issue with its plan so that you can reset to this spot if the implementation (step 3) isn’t what you want.<br/>3. **Ask Claude to implement its solution in code**. This is also a good place to ask it to explicitly verify the reasonableness of its solution as it implements pieces of the solution.<br/>4. **Ask Claude to commit the result and create a pull request**. If relevant, this is also a good time to have Claude update any READMEs or changelogs with an explanation of what it just did.<br/><br/>Steps #1-#2 are crucial—without them, Claude tends to jump straight to coding a solution. While sometimes that's what you want, asking Claude to research and plan first significantly improves performance for problems requiring deeper thinking upfront.<br/><br/>### b. Write tests, commit; code, iterate, commit<br/><br/>This is an Anthropic-favorite workflow for changes that are easily verifiable with unit, integration, or end-to-end tests. Test-driven development (TDD) becomes even more powerful with agentic coding:<br/><br/>1. **Ask Claude to write tests based on expected input/output pairs**. Be explicit about the fact that you’re doing test-driven development so that it avoids creating mock implementations, even for functionality that doesn’t exist yet in the codebase.<br/>2. **Tell Claude to run the tests and confirm they fail**. Explicitly telling it not to write any implementation code at this stage is often helpful.<br/>3. **Ask Claude to commit the tests** when you’re satisfied with them.<br/>4. **Ask Claude to write code that passes the tests**, instructing it not to modify the tests. Tell Claude to keep going until all tests pass. It will usually take a few iterations for Claude to write code, run the tests, adjust the code, and run the tests again.  <br/>   1. At this stage, it can help to ask it to verify with independent subagents that the implementation isn’t overfitting to the tests<br/>5. **Ask Claude to commit the code** once you’re satisfied with the changes.<br/><br/>Claude performs best when it has a clear target to iterate against—a visual mock, a test case, or another kind of output. By providing expected outputs like tests, Claude can make changes, evaluate results, and incrementally improve until it succeeds.<br/><br/>### c. Write code, screenshot result, iterate<br/><br/>Similar to the testing workflow, you can provide Claude with visual targets:<br/><br/>1. **Give Claude a way to take browser screenshots** (e.g., with the Puppeteer MCP server, an iOS simulator MCP server, or manually copy / paste screenshots into Claude).<br/>2. **Give Claude a visual mock** by copying / pasting or drag-dropping an image, or giving Claude the image file path.<br/>3. **Ask Claude to implement the design** in code, take screenshots of the result, and iterate until its result matches the mock.<br/>4. **Ask Claude to commit** when you're satisfied.<br/><br/>Like humans, Claude's outputs tend to improve significantly with iteration. While the first version might be good, after 2-3 iterations it will typically look much better. Give Claude the tools to see its outputs for best results.<br/><br/>Safe yolo mode<br/><br/>### d. Safe YOLO mode<br/><br/>Instead of supervising Claude, you can use `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` to bypass all permission checks and let Claude work uninterrupted until completion. This works well for workflows like fixing lint errors or generating boilerplate code.<br/><br/>Letting Claude run arbitrary commands is risky and can result in data loss, system corruption, or even data exfiltration (e.g., via prompt injection attacks). To minimize these risks, use `--dangerously-skip-permissions` in a container without internet access. You can follow this reference implementation using Docker Dev Containers.<br/><br/>### e. Codebase Q&A<br/><br/>When onboarding to a new codebase, use Claude Code for learning and exploration. You can ask Claude the same sorts of questions you would ask another engineer on the project when pair programming. Claude can agentically search the codebase to answer general questions like:<br/><br/>* How does logging work?<br/>* How do I make a new API endpoint?<br/>* What does `async move \{ ... \}` do on line 134 of `foo.rs`?<br/>* What edge cases does `CustomerOnboardingFlowImpl` handle?<br/>* Why are we calling `foo()` instead of `bar()` on line 333?<br/>* What’s the equivalent of line 334 of `baz.py` in Java?<br/><br/>At Anthropic, using Claude Code in this way has become our core onboarding workflow, significantly improving ramp-up time and reducing load on other engineers. No special prompting is required! Simply ask questions, and Claude will explore the code to find answers.<br/><br/>Use Claude to interact with git<br/><br/>### f. Use Claude to interact with git<br/><br/>Claude can effectively handle many git operations. Many Anthropic engineers use Claude for 90%+ of our _git_ interactions:<br/><br/>* **Searching _git_ history** to answer questions like "What changes made it into v1.2.3?", "Who owns this particular feature?", or "Why was this API designed this way?" It helps to explicitly prompt Claude to look through git history to answer queries like these.<br/>* **Writing commit messages**.Claude will look at your changes and recent history automatically to compose a message taking all the relevant context into account<br/>* **Handling complex git operations** like reverting files, resolving rebase conflicts, and comparing and grafting patches<br/><br/>### g. Use Claude to interact with GitHub<br/><br/>Claude Code can manage many GitHub interactions:<br/><br/>* **Creating pull requests**: Claude understands the shorthand "pr" and will generate appropriate commit messages based on the diff and surrounding context.<br/>* **Implementing one-shot resolutions** for simple code review comments: just tell it to fix comments on your PR (optionally, give it more specific instructions) and push back to the PR branch when it's done.<br/>* **Fixing failing builds** or linter warnings<br/>* **Categorizing and triaging open issues** by asking Claude to loop over open GitHub issues<br/><br/>This eliminates the need to remember `gh` command line syntax while automating routine tasks.<br/><br/>### h. Use Claude to work with Jupyter notebooks<br/><br/>Researchers and data scientists at Anthropic use Claude Code to read and write Jupyter notebooks. Claude can interpret outputs, including images, providing a fast way to explore and interact with data. There are no required prompts or workflows, but a workflow we recommend is to have Claude Code and a `.ipynb` file open side-by-side in VS Code.<br/><br/>You can also ask Claude to clean up or make aesthetic improvements to your Jupyter notebook before you show it to colleagues. Specifically telling it to make the notebook or its data visualizations “aesthetically pleasing” tends to help remind it that it’s optimizing for a human viewing experience.<br/><br/>## 4\. Optimize your workflow<br/><br/>The suggestions below apply across all workflows:<br/><br/>### a. Be specific in your instructions<br/><br/>Claude Code’s success rate improves significantly with more specific instructions, especially on first attempts. Giving clear directions upfront reduces the need for course corrections later.<br/><br/>For example:<br/><br/>\| Poor                                             \| Good                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    \|<br/>\| ------------------------------------------------ \| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- \|<br/>\| add tests for foo.py                             \| write a new test case for foo.py, covering the edge case where the user is logged out. avoid mocks                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      \|<br/>\| why does ExecutionFactory have such a weird api? \| look through ExecutionFactory's git history and summarize how its api came to be                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        \|<br/>\| add a calendar widget                            \| look at how existing widgets are implemented on the home page to understand the patterns and specifically how code and interfaces are separated out. HotDogWidget.php is a good example to start with. then, follow the pattern to implement a new calendar widget that lets the user select a month and paginate forwards/backwards to pick a year. Build from scratch without libraries other than the ones already used in the rest of the codebase. \|<br/><br/>Claude can infer intent, but it can't read minds. Specificity leads to better alignment with expectations.<br/><br/>Give Claude images<br/><br/>### b. Give Claude images<br/><br/>Claude excels with images and diagrams through several methods:<br/><br/>* **Paste screenshots** (pro tip: hit _cmd+ctrl+shift+4_ in macOS to screenshot to clipboard and _ctrl+v_ to paste. Note that this is not cmd+v like you would usually use to paste on mac and does not work remotely.)<br/>* **Drag and drop** images directly into the prompt input<br/>* **Provide file paths** for images<br/><br/>This is particularly useful when working with design mocks as reference points for UI development, and visual charts for analysis and debugging. If you are not adding visuals to context, it can still be helpful to be clear with Claude about how important it is for the result to be visually appealing.<br/><br/>Mention files you want Claude to look at or work on<br/><br/>### c. Mention files you want Claude to look at or work on<br/><br/>Use tab-completion to quickly reference files or folders anywhere in your repository, helping Claude find or update the right resources.<br/><br/>Give Claude URLs<br/><br/>### d. Give Claude URLs<br/><br/>Paste specific URLs alongside your prompts for Claude to fetch and read. To avoid permission prompts for the same domains (e.g., docs.foo.com), use`/permissions` to add domains to your allowlist.<br/><br/>### e. Course correct early and often<br/><br/>While auto-accept mode (shift+tab to toggle) lets Claude work autonomously, you'll typically get better results by being an active collaborator and guiding Claude's approach. You can get the best results by thoroughly explaining the task to Claude at the beginning, but you can also course correct Claude at any time.<br/><br/>These four tools help with course correction:<br/><br/>* **Ask Claude to make a plan** before coding. Explicitly tell it not to code until you’ve confirmed its plan looks good.<br/>* **Press Escape to interrupt** Claude during any phase (thinking, tool calls, file edits), preserving context so you can redirect or expand instructions.<br/>* **Double-tap Escape to jump back in history**, edit a previous prompt, and explore a different direction. You can edit the prompt and repeat until you get the result you're looking for.<br/>* **Ask Claude to undo changes**, often in conjunction with option #2 to take a different approach.<br/><br/>Though Claude Code occasionally solves problems perfectly on the first attempt, using these correction tools generally produces better solutions faster.<br/><br/>### f. Use `/clear` to keep context focused<br/><br/>During long sessions, Claude's context window can fill with irrelevant conversation, file contents, and commands. This can reduce performance and sometimes distract Claude. Use the `/clear` command frequently between tasks to reset the context window.<br/><br/>### g. Use checklists and scratchpads for complex workflows<br/><br/>For large tasks with multiple steps or requiring exhaustive solutions—like code migrations, fixing numerous lint errors, or running complex build scripts—improve performance by having Claude use a Markdown file (or even a GitHub issue!) as a checklist and working scratchpad:<br/><br/>For example, to fix a large number of lint issues, you can do the following:<br/><br/>1. **Tell Claude to run the lint command** and write all resulting errors (with filenames and line numbers) to a Markdown checklist<br/>2. **Instruct Claude to address each issue one by one**, fixing and verifying before checking it off and moving to the next<br/><br/>### h. Pass data into Claude<br/><br/>Several methods exist for providing data to Claude:<br/><br/>* **Copy and paste** directly into your prompt (most common approach)<br/>* **Pipe into Claude Code** (e.g., `cat foo.txt \| claude`), particularly useful for logs, CSVs, and large data<br/>* **Tell Claude to pull data** via bash commands, MCP tools, or custom slash commands<br/>* **Ask Claude to read files** or fetch URLs (works for images too)<br/><br/>Most sessions involve a combination of these approaches. For example, you can pipe in a log file, then tell Claude to use a tool to pull in additional context to debug the logs.<br/><br/>## 5\. Use headless mode to automate your infra<br/><br/>Claude Code includes headless mode for non-interactive contexts like CI, pre-commit hooks, build scripts, and automation. Use the `-p` flag with a prompt to enable headless mode, and `--output-format stream-json` for streaming JSON output.<br/><br/>Note that headless mode does not persist between sessions. You have to trigger it each session.<br/><br/>### a. Use Claude for issue triage<br/><br/>Headless mode can power automations triggered by GitHub events, such as when a new issue is created in your repository. For example, the public Claude Code repository uses Claude to inspect new issues as they come in and assign appropriate labels.<br/><br/>### b. Use Claude as a linter<br/><br/>Claude Code can provide subjective code reviews beyond what traditional linting tools detect, identifying issues like typos, stale comments, misleading function or variable names, and more.<br/><br/>## 6\. Uplevel with multi-Claude workflows<br/><br/>Beyond standalone usage, some of the most powerful applications involve running multiple Claude instances in parallel:<br/><br/>### a. Have one Claude write code; use another Claude to verify<br/><br/>A simple but effective approach is to have one Claude write code while another reviews or tests it. Similar to working with multiple engineers, sometimes having separate context is beneficial:<br/><br/>1. Use Claude to write code<br/>2. Run `/clear` or start a second Claude in another terminal<br/>3. Have the second Claude review the first Claude's work<br/>4. Start another Claude (or `/clear` again) to read both the code and review feedback<br/>5. Have this Claude edit the code based on the feedback<br/><br/>You can do something similar with tests: have one Claude write tests, then have another Claude write code to make the tests pass. You can even have your Claude instances communicate with each other by giving them separate working scratchpads and telling them which one to write to and which one to read from.<br/><br/>This separation often yields better results than having a single Claude handle everything.<br/><br/>### b. Have multiple checkouts of your repo<br/><br/>Rather than waiting for Claude to complete each step, something many engineers at Anthropic do is:<br/><br/>1. **Create 3-4 git checkouts** in separate folders<br/>2. **Open each folder** in separate terminal tabs<br/>3. **Start Claude in each folder** with different tasks<br/>4. **Cycle through** to check progress and approve/deny permission requests<br/><br/>### c. Use git worktrees<br/><br/>This approach shines for multiple independent tasks, offering a lighter-weight alternative to multiple checkouts. Git worktrees allow you to check out multiple branches from the same repository into separate directories. Each worktree has its own working directory with isolated files, while sharing the same Git history and reflog.<br/><br/>Using git worktrees enables you to run multiple Claude sessions simultaneously on different parts of your project, each focused on its own independent task. For instance, you might have one Claude refactoring your authentication system while another builds a completely unrelated data visualization component. Since the tasks don't overlap, each Claude can work at full speed without waiting for the other's changes or dealing with merge conflicts:<br/><br/>1. **Create worktrees**: `git worktree add ../project-feature-a feature-a`<br/>2. **Launch Claude in each worktree**: `cd ../project-feature-a && claude`<br/>3. **Create additional worktrees** as needed (repeat steps 1-2 in new terminal tabs)<br/><br/>Some tips:<br/><br/>* Use consistent naming conventions<br/>* Maintain one terminal tab per worktree<br/>* If you’re using iTerm2 on Mac, set up notifications for when Claude needs attention<br/>* Use separate IDE windows for different worktrees<br/>* Clean up when finished: `git worktree remove ../project-feature-a`<br/><br/>### d. Use headless mode with a custom harness<br/><br/>`claude -p` (headless mode) integrates Claude Code programmatically into larger workflows while leveraging its built-in tools and system prompt. There are two primary patterns for using headless mode:<br/><br/>1\. **Fanning out** handles large migrations or analyses (e.g., analyzing sentiment in hundreds of logs or analyzing thousands of CSVs):<br/><br/>1. Have Claude write a script to generate a task list. For example, generate a list of 2k files that need to be migrated from framework A to framework B.<br/>2. Loop through tasks, calling Claude programmatically for each and giving it a task and a set of tools it can use. For example: `claude -p “migrate foo.py from React to Vue. When you are done, you MUST return the string OK if you succeeded, or FAIL if the task failed.” --allowedTools Edit Bash(git commit:*)`<br/>3. Run the script several times and refine your prompt to get the desired outcome.<br/><br/>2\. **Pipelining** integrates Claude into existing data/processing pipelines:<br/><br/>1. Call `claude -p “<your prompt>” --json \| your_command`, where `your_command` is the next step of your processing pipeline<br/>2. That’s it! JSON output (optional) can help provide structure for easier automated processing.<br/><br/>For both of these use cases, it can be helpful to use the `--verbose` flag for debugging the Claude invocation. We generally recommend turning verbose mode off in production for cleaner output.<br/><br/>What are your tips and best practices for working with Claude Code? Tag @AnthropicAI so we can see what you're building!<br/><br/>## Acknowledgements<br/><br/>Written by Boris Cherny. This work draws upon best practices from across the broader Claude Code user community, whose creative approaches and workflows continue to inspire us. Special thanks also to Daisy Hollman, Ashwin Bhat, Cat Wu, Sid Bidasaria, Cal Rueb, Nodir Turakulov, Barry Zhang, Drew Hodun and many other Anthropic engineers whose valuable insights and practical experience with Claude Code helped shape these recommendations.<br/><br/>### Product<br/><br/>* Claude overview<br/>* Claude Code<br/>* Claude team plan<br/>* Claude enterprise plan<br/>* Claude education plan<br/>* Download Claude apps<br/>* Claude.ai pricing plans<br/>* Claude.ai login<br/><br/>### API Platform<br/><br/>* API overview<br/>* Developer docs<br/>* Claude in Amazon Bedrock<br/>* Claude on Google Cloud's Vertex AI<br/>* Pricing<br/>* Console login<br/><br/>### Research<br/><br/>* Research overview<br/>* Economic Index<br/><br/>### Claude models<br/><br/>* Claude Opus 4<br/>* Claude Sonnet 4<br/>* Claude Haiku 3.5<br/><br/>### Commitments<br/><br/>* Transparency<br/>* Responsible scaling policy<br/>* Security and compliance<br/><br/>### Solutions<br/><br/>* AI agents<br/>* Coding<br/>* Customer support<br/><br/>### Learn<br/><br/>* Anthropic Academy<br/>* Customer stories<br/>* Engineering at Anthropic<br/><br/>### Explore<br/><br/>* About us<br/>* Become a partner<br/>* Careers<br/>* Events<br/>* News<br/>* Startups program<br/><br/>### Help and security<br/><br/>* Status<br/>* Availability<br/>* Support center<br/><br/>### Terms and policies<br/><br/>Privacy choices<br/>* Privacy policy<br/>* Responsible disclosure policy<br/>* Terms of service - consumer<br/>* Terms of service - commercial<br/>* Usage policy<br/><br/>© 2025 Anthropic PBC |

Embeddings<br/>* Vision<br/>* PDF support<br/>* Files API<br/>* Google Sheets add-on<br/><br/>##### Tools<br/><br/>* Overview<br/>* How to implement tool use<br/>* Token-efficient tool use<br/>* Bash tool<br/>* Code execution tool<br/>* Computer use tool<br/>* Text editor tool<br/>* Web search tool<br/><br/>##### Model Context Protocol (MCP)<br/><br/>* MCP overview<br/>* MCP connector<br/>* Remote MCP servers<br/><br/>##### Test & evaluate<br/><br/>* Define success criteria<br/>* Develop test cases<br/>* Strengthen guardrails<br/>* Using the Evaluation Tool<br/><br/>##### Legal center<br/><br/>* Anthropic Privacy Policy<br/>* Security and compliance<br/><br/>Prompt engineering<br/><br/># Long context prompting tips<br/><br/>Copy page<br/><br/>While these tips apply broadly to all Claude models, you can find prompting tips specific to extended thinking models here.<br/><br/>Claude’s extended context window (200K tokens for Claude 3 models) enables handling complex, data-rich tasks. This guide will help you leverage this power effectively.<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Essential tips for long context prompts<br/><br/>* **Put longform data at the top**: Place your long documents and inputs (\~20K+ tokens) near the top of your prompt, above your query, instructions, and examples. This can significantly improve Claude’s performance across all models.  <br/>Queries at the end can improve response quality by up to 30% in tests, especially with complex, multi-document inputs.<br/>* **Structure document content and metadata with XML tags**: When using multiple documents, wrap each document in `<document>` tags with `<document_content>` and `<source>` (and other metadata) subtags for clarity.  <br/>Example multi-document structure  <br/>```xml  <br/><documents>  <br/>  <document index="1">  <br/>    <source>annual_report_2023.pdf</source>  <br/>    <document_content>  <br/>      \{{ANNUAL_REPORT\}}  <br/>    </document_content>  <br/>  </document>  <br/>  <document index="2">  <br/>    <source>competitor_analysis_q2.xlsx</source>  <br/>    <document_content>  <br/>      \{{COMPETITOR_ANALYSIS\}}  <br/>    </document_content>  <br/>  </document>  <br/></documents>  <br/>Analyze the annual report and competitor analysis. Identify strategic advantages and recommend Q3 focus areas.  <br/>```<br/>* **Ground responses in quotes**: For long document tasks, ask Claude to quote relevant parts of the documents first before carrying out its task. This helps Claude cut through the “noise” of the rest of the document’s contents.  <br/>Example quote extraction  <br/>```xml  <br/>You are an AI physician's assistant. Your task is to help doctors diagnose possible patient illnesses.  <br/><documents>  <br/>  <document index="1">  <br/>    <source>patient_symptoms.txt</source>  <br/>    <document_content>  <br/>      \{{PATIENT_SYMPTOMS\}}  <br/>    </document_content>  <br/>  </document>  <br/>  <document index="2">  <br/>    <source>patient_records.txt</source>  <br/>    <document_content>  <br/>      \{{PATIENT_RECORDS\}}  <br/>    </document_content>  <br/>  </document>  <br/>  <document index="3">  <br/>    <source>patient01_appt_history.txt</source>  <br/>    <document_content>  <br/>      \{{PATIENT01_APPOINTMENT_HISTORY\}}  <br/>    </document_content>  <br/>  </document>  <br/></documents>  <br/>Find quotes from the patient records and appointment history that are relevant to diagnosing the patient's reported symptoms. Place these in <quotes> tags. Then, based on these quotes, list all information that would help the doctor diagnose the patient's symptoms. Place your diagnostic information in <info> tags.  <br/>```<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>Prompt libraryGet inspired by a curated selection of prompts for various tasks and use cases.GitHub prompting tutorialAn example-filled tutorial that covers the prompt engineering concepts found in our docs.Google Sheets prompting tutorialA lighter weight version of our prompt engineering tutorial via an interactive spreadsheet.<br/><br/>Was this page helpful?<br/><br/>YesNo<br/><br/>Chain complex promptsExtended thinking tips<br/><br/>xlinkedindiscord<br/><br/>On this page<br/><br/>* Essential tips for long context prompts |

The script is no longer maintained because my claude account is not working properly.<br/><br/># Claude Prompt Enhancer<br/><br/>English \| 中文<br/><br/>The Claude Prompt Enhancer allows you to quickly insert predefined prompts when using Claude. Simply type a slash command followed by a space in the input box, and the corresponding prompt will automatically expand. For example, typing `/commit` and pressing the space will replace it with `Please help me write a Git commit message with the content:`.<br/><br/>Click here to install<br/><br/>\| Input command /commit                                                                                           \| Generated Prompt                                                                                              \|<br/>\| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- \| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- \|<br/>\| [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/FishHawk/claude-prompt-enhancer/main/.github/screenshot/before.webp) \| [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/FishHawk/claude-prompt-enhancer/main/.github/screenshot/after.webp) \|<br/><br/>Setting button:<br/><br/>button<br/><br/>Prompt command editor:<br/><br/>editor<br/><br/>## About<br/><br/> Use predefined prompts in Claude by custom command<br/><br/>greasyfork.org/zh-CN/scripts/489838 <br/><br/>### Topics<br/><br/> userscript  prompt  claude <br/><br/>### Resources<br/><br/> Readme <br/><br/>### License<br/><br/> GPL-3.0 license <br/><br/>###  Uh oh!<br/><br/>There was an error while loading. | For Claude Desktop Users - Model Context Protocol | https://modelcontextprotocol.io/quickstart/user | Model Context Protocol home pagelight logodark logo<br/><br/>Search...<br/><br/>⌘K<br/><br/>* C# SDK<br/>* Java SDK<br/>* Kotlin SDK<br/>* Python SDK<br/>* Ruby SDK<br/>* Swift SDK<br/>* TypeScript SDK<br/><br/>##### Get Started<br/><br/>* Introduction<br/>* Quickstart  <br/>   * For Server Developers  <br/>   * For Client Developers  <br/>   * For Claude Desktop Users<br/>* Example Servers<br/>* Example Clients<br/>* FAQs<br/><br/>##### Tutorials<br/><br/>* Building MCP with LLMs<br/>* Debugging<br/>* Inspector<br/><br/>##### Concepts<br/><br/>* Core architecture<br/>* Resources<br/>* Prompts<br/>* Tools<br/>* Sampling<br/>* Roots<br/>* Transports<br/><br/>##### Development<br/><br/>* Roadmap<br/>* Contributing<br/><br/>Model Context Protocol home pagelight logodark logo<br/><br/>Search...<br/><br/>⌘K<br/><br/>* GitHub<br/>* GitHub<br/><br/>Search...<br/><br/>Navigation<br/><br/>Quickstart<br/><br/>For Claude Desktop Users<br/><br/>User GuideSDKsSpecification<br/><br/>User GuideSDKsSpecification<br/><br/>* GitHub<br/><br/>Quickstart<br/><br/># For Claude Desktop Users<br/><br/>Copy page<br/><br/>Get started using pre-built servers in Claude for Desktop.<br/><br/>In this tutorial, you will extend Claude for Desktop so that it can read from your computer’s file system, write new files, move files, and even search files.<br/><br/>Don’t worry — it will ask you for your permission before executing these actions!<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>1\. Download Claude for Desktop<br/><br/>Start by downloading Claude for Desktop, choosing either macOS or Windows. (Linux is not yet supported for Claude for Desktop.)<br/><br/>Follow the installation instructions.<br/><br/>If you already have Claude for Desktop, make sure it’s on the latest version by clicking on the Claude menu on your computer and selecting “Check for Updates…”<br/><br/>Why Claude for Desktop and not Claude.ai?<br/><br/>Because servers are locally run, MCP currently only supports desktop hosts. Remote hosts are in active development.<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>2\. Add the Filesystem MCP Server<br/><br/>To add this filesystem functionality, we will be installing a pre-built Filesystem MCP Server to Claude for Desktop. This is one of several current reference servers and many community-created servers.<br/><br/>Get started by opening up the Claude menu on your computer and select “Settings…” Please note that these are not the Claude Account Settings found in the app window itself.<br/><br/>This is what it should look like on a Mac:<br/><br/>Click on “Developer” in the left-hand bar of the Settings pane, and then click on “Edit Config”:<br/><br/>This will create a configuration file at:<br/><br/>* macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json`<br/>* Windows: `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json`<br/><br/>if you don’t already have one, and will display the file in your file system.<br/><br/>Open up the configuration file in any text editor. Replace the file contents with this:<br/><br/>* MacOS/Linux<br/>* Windows<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>\{<br/>  "mcpServers": \{<br/>    "filesystem": \{<br/>      "command": "npx",<br/>      "args": [<br/>        "-y",<br/>        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem",<br/>        "/Users/username/Desktop",<br/>        "/Users/username/Downloads"<br/>      ]<br/>    \}<br/>  \}<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>\{<br/>  "mcpServers": \{<br/>    "filesystem": \{<br/>      "command": "npx",<br/>      "args": [<br/>        "-y",<br/>        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem",<br/>        "/Users/username/Desktop",<br/>        "/Users/username/Downloads"<br/>      ]<br/>    \}<br/>  \}<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>\{<br/>  "mcpServers": \{<br/>    "filesystem": \{<br/>      "command": "npx",<br/>      "args": [<br/>        "-y",<br/>        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem",<br/>        "C:\\Users\\username\\Desktop",<br/>        "C:\\Users\\username\\Downloads"<br/>      ]<br/>    \}<br/>  \}<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>Make sure to replace `username` with your computer’s username. The paths should point to valid directories that you want Claude to be able to access and modify. It’s set up to work for Desktop and Downloads, but you can add more paths as well.<br/><br/>You will also need Node.js on your computer for this to run properly. To verify you have Node installed, open the command line on your computer.<br/><br/>* On macOS, open the Terminal from your Applications folder<br/>* On Windows, press Windows + R, type “cmd”, and press Enter<br/><br/>Once in the command line, verify you have Node installed by entering in the following command:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>node --version<br/>```<br/><br/>If you get an error saying “command not found” or “node is not recognized”, download Node from nodejs.org.<br/><br/>**How does the configuration file work?**<br/><br/>This configuration file tells Claude for Desktop which MCP servers to start up every time you start the application. In this case, we have added one server called “filesystem” that will use the Node `npx` command to install and run `@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem`. This server, described here, will let you access your file system in Claude for Desktop.<br/><br/>**Command Privileges**<br/><br/>Claude for Desktop will run the commands in the configuration file with the permissions of your user account, and access to your local files. Only add commands if you understand and trust the source.<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>3\. Restart Claude<br/><br/>After updating your configuration file, you need to restart Claude for Desktop.<br/><br/>Upon restarting, you should see a slider  icon in the bottom left corner of the input box:<br/><br/>After clicking on the slider icon, you should see the tools that come with the Filesystem MCP Server:<br/><br/>If your server isn’t being picked up by Claude for Desktop, proceed to the Troubleshooting section for debugging tips.<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>4\. Try it out!<br/><br/>You can now talk to Claude and ask it about your filesystem. It should know when to call the relevant tools.<br/><br/>Things you might try asking Claude:<br/><br/>* Can you write a poem and save it to my desktop?<br/>* What are some work-related files in my downloads folder?<br/>* Can you take all the images on my desktop and move them to a new folder called “Images”?<br/><br/>As needed, Claude will call the relevant tools and seek your approval before taking an action:<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Troubleshooting<br/><br/>Server not showing up in Claude / hammer icon missing<br/><br/>1. Restart Claude for Desktop completely<br/>2. Check your `claude_desktop_config.json` file syntax<br/>3. Make sure the file paths included in `claude_desktop_config.json` are valid and that they are absolute and not relative<br/>4. Look at logs to see why the server is not connecting<br/>5. In your command line, try manually running the server (replacing `username` as you did in `claude_desktop_config.json`) to see if you get any errors:<br/><br/>* MacOS/Linux<br/>* Windows<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /Users/username/Desktop /Users/username/Downloads<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /Users/username/Desktop /Users/username/Downloads<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem C:\Users\username\Desktop C:\Users\username\Downloads<br/>```<br/><br/>Getting logs from Claude for Desktop<br/><br/>Claude.app logging related to MCP is written to log files in:<br/><br/>* macOS: `~/Library/Logs/Claude`<br/>* Windows: `%APPDATA%\Claude\logs`<br/>* `mcp.log` will contain general logging about MCP connections and connection failures.<br/>* Files named `mcp-server-SERVERNAME.log` will contain error (stderr) logging from the named server.<br/><br/>You can run the following command to list recent logs and follow along with any new ones (on Windows, it will only show recent logs):<br/><br/>* MacOS/Linux<br/>* Windows<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/># Check Claude's logs for errors<br/>tail -n 20 -f ~/Library/Logs/Claude/mcp*.log<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/># Check Claude's logs for errors<br/>tail -n 20 -f ~/Library/Logs/Claude/mcp*.log<br/>```<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>type "%APPDATA%\Claude\logs\mcp*.log"<br/>```<br/><br/>Tool calls failing silently<br/><br/>If Claude attempts to use the tools but they fail:<br/><br/>1. Check Claude’s logs for errors<br/>2. Verify your server builds and runs without errors<br/>3. Try restarting Claude for Desktop<br/><br/>None of this is working. What do I do?<br/><br/>Please refer to our debugging guide for better debugging tools and more detailed guidance.<br/><br/>ENOENT error and \`$\{APPDATA\}\` in paths on Windows<br/><br/>If your configured server fails to load, and you see within its logs an error referring to `$\{APPDATA\}` within a path, you may need to add the expanded value of `%APPDATA%` to your `env` key in `claude_desktop_config.json`:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>\{<br/>  "brave-search": \{<br/>    "command": "npx",<br/>    "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-brave-search"],<br/>    "env": \{<br/>      "APPDATA": "C:\\Users\\user\\AppData\\Roaming\\",<br/>      "BRAVE_API_KEY": "..."<br/>    \}<br/>  \}<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/>With this change in place, launch Claude Desktop once again.<br/><br/>**NPM should be installed globally**<br/><br/>The `npx` command may continue to fail if you have not installed NPM globally. If NPM is already installed globally, you will find `%APPDATA%\npm` exists on your system. If not, you can install NPM globally by running the following command:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>npm install -g npm<br/>```<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Next steps<br/><br/>Explore other serversCheck out our gallery of official MCP servers and implementationsBuild your own serverNow build your own custom server to use in Claude for Desktop and other clients<br/><br/>Was this page helpful?<br/><br/>YesNo<br/><br/>For Client DevelopersExample Servers<br/><br/>github<br/><br/>On this page<br/><br/>* 1\. Download Claude for Desktop<br/>* 2\. Add the Filesystem MCP Server<br/>* 3\. Restart Claude<br/>* 4\. Try it out!<br/>* Troubleshooting<br/>* Next steps<br/><br/>Assistant<br/><br/>Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes. |

Dismiss alert <br/><br/>\{{ message \}}<br/><br/> Instantly share code, notes, and snippets.<br/><br/>@sarath-menon <br/><br/># sarath-menon/**claude\_code\_prompt.md** <br/><br/> CreatedMarch 2, 2025 21:42 <br/><br/>Show Gist options <br/><br/>* Download ZIP<br/><br/>* Star 9 (9) You must be signed in to star a gist<br/>* Fork 7 (7) You must be signed in to fork a gist<br/><br/>* Embed  <br/>   * Embed Embed this gist in your website.  <br/>   * Share Copy sharable link for this gist.  <br/>   * Clone via HTTPS Clone using the web URL.  <br/>   * Learn more about clone URLs  <br/> Clone this repository at &lt;script src=&quot;https://gist.github.com/sarath-menon/1ca2fcd19fb2e0ed53b8ef50c3540ea0.js&quot;&gt;&lt;/script&gt;<br/>* Save sarath-menon/1ca2fcd19fb2e0ed53b8ef50c3540ea0 to your computer and use it in GitHub Desktop.<br/><br/> Code  Revisions1  Stars9  Forks7 <br/><br/>Embed <br/><br/>* Embed Embed this gist in your website.<br/>* Share Copy sharable link for this gist.<br/>* Clone via HTTPS Clone using the web URL.<br/>* Learn more about clone URLs<br/><br/> Clone this repository at &lt;script src=&quot;https://gist.github.com/sarath-menon/1ca2fcd19fb2e0ed53b8ef50c3540ea0.js&quot;&gt;&lt;/script&gt; <br/><br/>Save sarath-menon/1ca2fcd19fb2e0ed53b8ef50c3540ea0 to your computer and use it in GitHub Desktop. <br/><br/>Download ZIP <br/><br/>Raw <br/><br/> **claude\_code\_prompt.md** <br/><br/># Code Bash command prefix detection<br/><br/>This defines risk levels for actions that the $\{K4\} agent may take. This classification system is part of a broader safety framework and is used to determine when additional user confirmation or oversight may be needed.<br/><br/>## Command prefix extraction examples<br/><br/>Examples:<br/><br/>* cat foo.txt => cat<br/>* cd src => cd<br/>* cd path/to/files/ => cd<br/>* find ./src -type f -name "\*.ts" => find<br/>* gg cat foo.py => gg cat<br/>* gg cp foo.py bar.py => gg cp<br/>* git commit -m "foo" => git commit<br/>* git diff HEAD\~1 => git diff<br/>* git diff --staged => git diff<br/>* git diff $(pwd) => command\_injection\_detected<br/>* git status => git status<br/>* git status# test(\`id\`) => command\_injection\_detected<br/>* git status\`ls\` => command\_injection\_detected<br/>* git push => none<br/>* git push origin master => git push<br/>* git log -n 5 => git log<br/>* git log --oneline -n 5 => git log<br/>* grep -A 40 "from foo.bar.baz import" alpha/beta/gamma.py => grep<br/>* pig tail zerba.log => pig tail<br/>* notion test some/specific/file.ts => notion test<br/>* npm test => none<br/>* npm test --foo => npm test<br/>* npm test -- -f "foo" => npm test<br/>* pwd curl example.com => command\_injection\_detected<br/>* pytest foo/bar.py => pytest<br/>* scalac build => none </policy\_spec><br/><br/>The user has allowed certain command prefixes to be run, and will otherwise be asked to approve or deny the command. Your task is to determine the command prefix for the following command.<br/><br/>IMPORTANT: Bash commands may run multiple commands that are chained together. For safety, if the command seems to contain command injection, you must return "command\_injection\_detected". (This will help protect the user: if they think that they're allowlisting command A, but the AI coding agent sends a malicious command that technically has the same prefix as command A, then the safety system will see that you said “command\_injection\_detected” and ask the user for manual confirmation.)<br/><br/>Note that not every command has a prefix. If a command has no prefix, return "none".<br/><br/>ONLY return the prefix. Do not return any other text, markdown markers, or other content or formatting.<br/><br/>To detect command prefixes and prevent command injection in shell interactions:<br/><br/>* A function is used to analyze user input that might contain bash commands<br/>* Returns objects with properties like `commandPrefix` and `commandInjectionDetected`<br/>* Uses a pattern `pd` to detect command prefixes<br/>* Special handling for responses like "git", "none", and "command\_injection\_detected"<br/>* Returns `null` if the message content starts with the predefined prefix<br/><br/>## Command Validation and Security<br/><br/>**Code Description:**<br/><br/>* Creates a Set data structure containing potentially dangerous command operators ("&&", "\|\|", ";", ";;")<br/>* These operators could be used for command chaining which presents security risks<br/>* The system uses this set to identify and handle these operators specially during command validation<br/><br/>**Command Validation Function:**<br/><br/>* Parses shell commands using a specialized parser<br/>* Replaces quote characters with special markers to handle quoted content safely<br/>* Examines each parsed token to detect potentially unsafe operations<br/>* Returns a boolean indicating whether the command is considered safe<br/>* Specifically checks for operations other than globbing patterns and allowed operators<br/>* If any unsafe operations are found, the function returns false<br/><br/>**Command Injection Detection:**<br/><br/>* Analyzes commands to determine if they contain multiple parts that could indicate injection attempts<br/>* Checks command structure against known safe patterns<br/>* Uses the command validation function as part of its security assessment<br/>* Returns true if command injection is detected, allowing the system to take appropriate action<br/><br/>## File and Code Handling<br/><br/>The code includes comprehensive utilities for working with files:<br/><br/>* Functions for checking if files exist using `existsSync`<br/>* Path handling with functions from the `path` module like `extname`, `relative`<br/>* Code highlighting functionality for displaying code with syntax highlighting<br/>* Functions to truncate long content and handle different types of content<br/>* Special handling for image files and data URLs<br/><br/>## Path Management and Workspace Detection<br/><br/>**Path Checking Functions:**<br/><br/>* `ss` function: Determines if a given path is within the current workspace  <br/>   * Takes a path parameter and normalizes it using a helper function  <br/>   * Compares it with the normalized workspace root path  <br/>   * Returns true if the path starts with the workspace path, false otherwise<br/>* `hC` function: Checks if a path is within any trusted directory  <br/>   * Normalizes the input path for consistent comparison  <br/>   * Iterates through a collection of trusted directory paths  <br/>   * Returns true if the path Okay, the search results confirm a key aspect of how Claude Code works: **it automatically finds context from the files within your project directory.** You don't "attach" files in the traditional sense. Instead, you guide its attention.

Here are the primary ways to provide context:

1.  **Mention Files by Path:** The most direct way is to simply mention the file or folder you want it to look at. You can use tab-completion to get the correct path.
2.  **Give it URLs:** You can paste a URL directly into the prompt, and Claude will fetch and read its content.
3.  **Use `CLAUDE.md` Files:** This is a special file for providing persistent context. You can create a `CLAUDE.md` file in your project's root directory. Claude automatically reads this file at the start of every session. It's the perfect place to put:
    *   Project-specific build or test commands.
    *   Descriptions of your core files and utilities.
    *   Notes on code style or architecture.
4.  **Pipe Data into it:** For logs or other data, you can use the command line to pipe content directly into Claude Code (e.g., `cat server.log | claude`).
5.  **Paste Text or Images:** You can copy-paste text snippets or even screenshots directly into the interactive prompt.

+ # Claude Code: The Cliff Notes
+ 
+ This guide provides a quick, scannable overview of how to get the most out of Claude Code. It's for developers who have it installed and want to dive right into using it.
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 1. Overview: What is Claude Code?
+ 
+ Claude Code is a powerful, AI-driven coding assistant that lives in your terminal. Think of it as a pair programmer that understands your entire codebase. It's not just a chatbot; it's a tool that can take real action, from explaining complex code to refactoring, testing, and managing your Git workflow.
+ 
+ **Why it's a big deal:**
+ 
+ *   **Saves Time:** Automates repetitive tasks like writing boilerplate code, generating tests, and refactoring legacy systems.
+ *   **Deep Code Understanding:** It doesn't just see one file at a time; it analyzes your entire project to give you context-aware help.
+ *   **Terminal-Native:** It works where you work, integrating smoothly with your existing command-line tools like Git and Docker.
+ *   **Action-Oriented:** It can directly generate and modify code, fix bugs, and even create commits and pull requests for you.
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 2. Providing Context to Claude
+ 
+ You don't "attach" files to Claude Code in the traditional sense. Instead, you guide its attention to the context it needs. Here's how:
+ 
+ *   **Mention Files by Path:** This is the most common method. Simply reference the file or folder in your prompt. Use tab-completion to ensure the path is correct.
+     > `> what does `src/api/auth.js` do?`
+ 
+ *   **Use a `CLAUDE.md` File:** This is a special file for *persistent* context. Create a file named `CLAUDE.md` in your project's root directory. Claude automatically reads it at the start of every session. It's the perfect place for:
+     *   Project-specific build or test commands (`npm run test:unit`).
+     *   Notes on code style or architecture patterns.
+     *   A list of core utility files.
+ 
+ *   **Give it URLs:** Paste a URL directly into the prompt, and Claude will fetch and read the content. This is great for documentation or articles.
+ 
+ *   **Pipe Data from the Command Line:** You can pipe content directly into Claude Code. This is very useful for logs or other command outputs.
+     ```bash
+     cat server.log | claude "summarize these errors"
+     ```
+ 
+ *   **Paste Text or Images:** You can copy and paste text snippets or even screenshots (e.g., of a UI mock or a diagram) directly into the interactive prompt.
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 3. Getting Started: Your First Commands
+ 
+ Once you're in your project's directory, you can start working with Claude Code immediately.
+ 
+ **Launch Interactive Mode:**
+ 
+ Simply type the following command to enter an interactive session where you can have a conversation about your code:
+ 
+ ```bash
+ claude
+ ```
+ 
+ **Starter Commands (Interactive Mode):**
+ 
+ Once you see the `>` prompt, try these:
+ 
+ *   **Get a high-level overview of your project:**
+     ```
+     > give me an overview of this codebase
+     ```
+ *   **Ask specific questions about your architecture or logic:**
+     ```
+     > explain the main architecture patterns used here
+     > how is authentication handled in this project?
+     ```
+ *   **Generate a project guide:**
+     This creates a `CLAUDE.md` file that summarizes the project's architecture, key concepts, and common workflows—perfect for new team members.
+     ```
+     > /init
+     ```
+ 
+ **One-off Commands (From your shell):**
+ 
+ You can also use `claude-code` for quick tasks without starting an interactive session:
+ 
+ *   **Summarize the project:**
+     ```bash
+     claude-code "summarize this project"
+     ```
+ *   **Create a Git commit:**
+     This will analyze your staged changes and write a descriptive commit message.
+     ```bash
+     claude-code commit
+     ```
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 4. Core Features & How It Works
+ 
+ Claude Code's power comes from its ability to understand and interact with your code in sophisticated ways.
+ 
+ *   **Code Intelligence at Scale:**
+     Perform a "semantic search" that understands concepts, not just keywords.
+     ```bash
+     claude-code search "JWT token validation"
+     ```
+ *   **Automated Refactoring:**
+     Modernize legacy code or apply new patterns with a single command.
+     ```bash
+     claude-code refactor src/legacy/apiHandlers.js
+     ```
+ *   **AI-Driven Testing:**
+     Generate and run tests using your project's testing framework.
+     ```bash
+     claude-code test --framework jest src/modules/paymentProcessor.js
+     ```
+ *   **Git Automation:**
+     Handle complex Git workflows with simple, natural language commands.
+     ```sql
+     claude-code commit --type feat --scope auth --message "Add 2FA support"
+     ```
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 5. Advanced Workflows & Pro-Tips
+ 
+ To unlock the full potential of Claude Code, think in terms of workflows, not just single commands.
+ 
+ **Pro-Tip 1: It's a Powerful CLI Tool**
+ 
+ *   **Headless Mode:** Use the `-p` flag to run it without the interactive prompt.
+ *   **Chain Commands:** Combine it with other tools you already use (e.g., `git status | claude-code`).
+ *   **Piping:** Pipe data directly into it for analysis.
+ *   **Self-Spawning Agents:** When you assign a task, Claude Code often spins up sub-agents to work on it in parallel.
+ 
+ **Pro-Tip 2: Think Workflows, Not Snippets**
+ 
+ *   **Onboarding:** New developers can run `claude-code summarize` to get up to speed in minutes instead of days.
+ *   **CI/CD Integration:** Add Claude Code to your CI/CD pipeline for automated code reviews.
+     ```yaml
+     - name: AI Code Review
+       run: claude-code review --severity critical
+     ```
+ *   **Complex Debugging:** Use `claude-code trace` to follow logic across multiple services and identify tricky bugs.
+ 
+ **Pro-Tip 3: Guide its Reasoning**
+ 
+ *   For complex problems, ask it to "think" first. This encourages it to spend more time analyzing before giving a solution.
+     ```
+     > think hard about the edge cases in our authentication flow
+     > think about how we should architect the new payment service
+     ```
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 6. Use Cases & Practical Examples
+ 
+ Here's how these features come together in real-world scenarios.
+ 
+ *   **Understanding Legacy Code:**
+     You inherit a massive, undocumented codebase.
+     **Instead of:** Hours of `grep` and manually tracing function calls.
+     **With Claude Code:**
+     ```
+     > explain how the caching layer works
+     > find where user permissions are checked
+     > which commit added tests for markdown back in December?
+     ```
+ 
+ *   **Rapid Implementation:**
+     You need to add a new feature that touches multiple files.
+     **Instead of:** Manually editing each file and hoping you didn't miss anything.
+     **With Claude Code:**
+     ```
+     > add input validation to the signup form
+     > refactor the logger to use the new API
+     > fix the race condition in the worker queue
+     ```
+ 
+ *   **Complex Git Operations:**
+     You need to rebase your feature branch and resolve merge conflicts.
+     **Instead of:** A multi-step, manual Git dance.
+     **With Claude Code:**
+     ```
+     > rebase on main and resolve any merge conflicts
+     ``` 
```

## Pro Tips for Developers

**Precision Matters:

** Use file paths and line numbers for targeted fixes.

**Cost Control:

code commit`).

** Store API keys in `.env` files instead of hardcoding them.

For example, an allow rule with `Bash(git diff:*)` would allow Bash commands that start with `git diff`. The following tools support permission rules with specifiers:<br/><br/>#### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Bash<br/><br/>* `Bash(npm run build)` Matches the exact Bash command `npm run build`<br/>* `Bash(npm run test:*)` Matches Bash commands starting with `npm run test`.<br/><br/>Claude Code is aware of shell operators (like `&&`) so a prefix match rule like `Bash(safe-cmd:*)` won’t give it permission to run the command `safe-cmd && other-cmd`<br/><br/>#### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Read & Edit<br/><br/>`Edit` rules apply to all built-in tools that edit files. Claude will make a best-effort attempt to apply `Read` rules to all built-in tools that read files like Grep, Glob, and LS.<br/><br/>Read & Edit rules both follow thegitignore specification. Patterns are resolved relative to the directory containing `.claude/settings.json`. To reference an absolute path, use `//`. For a path relative to your home directory, use `~/`.<br/><br/>* `Edit(docs/**)` Matches edits to files in the `docs` directory of your project<br/>* `Read(~/.zshrc)` Matches reads to your `~/.zshrc` file<br/>* `Edit(//tmp/scratch.txt)` Matches edits to `/tmp/scratch.txt`<br/><br/>#### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>WebFetch<br/><br/>* `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` Matches fetch requests to example.com<br/><br/>#### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>MCP<br/><br/>* `mcp__puppeteer` Matches any tool provided by the `puppeteer` server (name configured in Claude Code)<br/>* `mcp__puppeteer__puppeteer_navigate` Matches the `puppeteer_navigate` tool provided by the `puppeteer` server<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Auto-updater permission options<br/><br/>When Claude Code detects that it doesn’t have sufficient permissions to write to your global npm prefix directory (required for automatic updates), you’ll see a warning that points to this documentation page. For detailed solutions to auto-updater issues, see thetroubleshooting guide.<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Recommended: Create a new user-writable npm prefix<br/><br/>```bash<br/># First, save a list of your existing global packages for later migration<br/>npm list -g --depth=0 > ~/npm-global-packages.txt<br/><br/># Create a directory for your global packages<br/>mkdir -p ~/.npm-global<br/><br/># Configure npm to use the new directory path<br/>npm config set prefix ~/.npm-global<br/><br/># Note: Replace ~/.bashrc with ~/.zshrc, ~/.profile, or other appropriate file for your shell<br/>echo 'export PATH=~/.npm-global/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc<br/><br/># Apply the new PATH setting<br/>source ~/.bashrc<br/><br/># Now reinstall Claude Code in the new location<br/>npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code<br/><br/># Optional: Reinstall your previous global packages in the new location<br/># Look at ~/npm-global-packages.txt and install packages you want to keep<br/># npm install -g package1 package2 package3...<br/><br/>```<br/><br/>**Why we recommend this option:**<br/><br/>* Avoids modifying system directory permissions<br/>* Creates a clean, dedicated location for your global npm packages<br/>* Follows security best practices<br/><br/>Since Claude Code is actively developing, we recommend setting up auto-updates using the recommended option above.<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Disabling the auto-updater<br/><br/>If you prefer to disable the auto-updater instead of fixing permissions, you can set the `DISABLE_AUTOUPDATER` environment variable to `1`<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Optimize your terminal setup<br/><br/>Claude Code works best when your terminal is properly configured. Follow these guidelines to optimize your experience.<br/><br/>**Supported shells**:<br/><br/>* Bash<br/>* Zsh<br/>* Fish<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Themes and appearance<br/><br/>Claude cannot control the theme of your terminal. That’s handled by your terminal application. Open Settings → Profiles → Keyboard<br/>2. Check “Use Option as Meta Key”<br/><br/>**For iTerm2 and VSCode terminal:**<br/><br/>1. Open Settings → Profiles → Keys<br/>2. Set your preferred notification delay<br/><br/>Note that these notifications are specific to iTerm 2 and not available in the default macOS Terminal.<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Handling large inputs<br/><br/>When working with extensive code or long instructions:<br/><br/>* **Avoid direct pasting**: Claude Code may struggle with very long pasted content<br/>* **Use file-based workflows**: Write content to a file and ask Claude to read it<br/>* **Be aware of VS Code limitations**: The VS Code terminal is particularly prone to truncating long pastes<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Vim Mode<br/><br/>Claude Code supports a subset of Vim keybindings that can be enabled with `/vim`or configured via `/config`.<br/><br/>The supported subset includes:<br/><br/>* Mode switching: `Esc` (to NORMAL), `i`/`I`, `a`/`A`, `o`/`O` (to INSERT)<br/>* Navigation: `h`/`j`/`k`/`l`, `w`/`e`/`b`, `0`/`$`/`^`, `gg`/`G`<br/>* Editing: `x`, `dw`/`de`/`db`/`dd`/`D`, `cw`/`ce`/`cb`/`cc`/`C`, `.` (repeat)<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Environment variables<br/><br/>Claude Code supports the following environment variables to control its behavior:<br/><br/>All environment variables can also be configured insettings.json. This is useful as a way to automatically set environment variables for each session, or to roll out a set of environment variables for your whole team or organization.<br/><br/>\| Variable                                     \| Purpose                                                                                                                              \|<br/>\| -------------------------------------------- \| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ \|<br/>\| ANTHROPIC\_API\_KEY                          \| API key sent as X-Api-Key header, typically for the Claude SDK (for interactive usage, run /login)                                   \|<br/>\| ANTHROPIC\_AUTH\_TOKEN                       \| Custom value for the Authorization and Proxy-Authorization headers (the value you set here will be prefixed with Bearer )            \|<br/>\| ANTHROPIC\_CUSTOM\_HEADERS                   \| Custom headers you want to add to the request (in Name: Value format)                                                                \|<br/>\| ANTHROPIC\_MODEL                             \| Name of custom model to use (see [Model Configuration](/en/docs/claude-code/bedrock-vertex-proxies#model-configuration))             \|<br/>\| ANTHROPIC\_SMALL\_FAST\_MODEL                \| Name of [Haiku-class model for background tasks](/en/docs/claude-code/costs)                                                         \|<br/>\| BASH\_DEFAULT\_TIMEOUT\_MS                   \| Default timeout for long-running bash commands                                                                                       \|<br/>\| BASH\_MAX\_TIMEOUT\_MS                       \| Maximum timeout the model can set for long-running bash commands                                                                     \|<br/>\| BASH\_MAX\_OUTPUT\_LENGTH                    \| Maximum number of characters in bash outputs before they are middle-truncated                                                        \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_API\_KEY\_HELPER\_TTL\_MS      \| Interval in milliseconds at which credentials should be refreshed (when using apiKeyHelper)                                          \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_MAX\_OUTPUT\_TOKENS            \| Set the maximum number of output tokens for most requests                                                                            \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_USE\_BEDROCK                   \| Use Bedrock (see [Bedrock & Vertex](/en/docs/claude-code/bedrock-vertex-proxies))                                                    \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_USE\_VERTEX                    \| Use Vertex (see [Bedrock & Vertex](/en/docs/claude-code/bedrock-vertex-proxies))                                                     \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_SKIP\_BEDROCK\_AUTH            \| Skip AWS authentication for Bedrock (e.g. when using an LLM gateway)                                                                 \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_SKIP\_VERTEX\_AUTH             \| Skip Google authentication for Vertex (e.g. when using an LLM gateway)                                                               \|<br/>\| CLAUDE\_CODE\_DISABLE\_NONESSENTIAL\_TRAFFIC \| Equivalent of setting DISABLE\_AUTOUPDATER, DISABLE\_BUG\_COMMAND, DISABLE\_ERROR\_REPORTING, and DISABLE\_TELEMETRY                 \|<br/>\| DISABLE\_AUTOUPDATER                         \| Set to 1 to disable the automatic updater                                                                                            \|<br/>\| DISABLE\_BUG\_COMMAND                        \| Set to 1 to disable the /bug command                                                                                                 \|<br/>\| DISABLE\_COST\_WARNINGS                      \| Set to 1 to disable cost warning messages                                                                                            \|<br/>\| DISABLE\_ERROR\_REPORTING                    \| Set to 1 to opt out of Sentry error reporting                                                                                        \|<br/>\| DISABLE\_NON\_ESSENTIAL\_MODEL\_CALLS        \| Set to 1 to disable model calls for non-critical paths like flavor text                                                              \|<br/>\| DISABLE\_TELEMETRY                           \| Set to 1 to opt out of Statsig telemetry (note that Statsig events do not include user data like code, file paths, or bash commands) \|<br/>\| HTTP\_PROXY                                  \| Specify HTTP proxy server for network connections                                                                                    \|<br/>\| HTTPS\_PROXY                                 \| Specify HTTPS proxy server for network connections                                                                                   \|<br/>\| MAX\_THINKING\_TOKENS                        \| Force a thinking for the model budget                                                                                                \|<br/>\| MCP\_TIMEOUT                                 \| Timeout in milliseconds for MCP server startup                                                                                       \|<br/>\| MCP\_TOOL\_TIMEOUT                           \| Timeout in milliseconds for MCP tool execution                                                                                       \|<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Configuration options<br/><br/>We are in the process of migration global configuration to `settings.json`.<br/><br/>`claude config` will be deprecated in place of settings.json<br/><br/>To manage your configurations, use the following commands:<br/><br/>* List settings: `claude config list`<br/>* See a setting: `claude config get <key>`<br/>* Change a setting: `claude config set <key> <value>`<br/>* Push to a setting (for lists): `claude config add <key> <value>`<br/>* Remove from a setting (for lists): `claude config remove <key> <value>`<br/><br/>By default `config` changes your project configuration. To manage your global configuration, use the `--global` (or `-g`) flag.<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>​<br/><br/>Global configuration<br/><br/>To set a global configuration, use `claude config set -g <key> <value>`:<br/><br/>\| Key                   \| Description                                                    \| Example                                                                \|<br/>\| --------------------- \| -------------------------------------------------------------- \| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- \|<br/>\| autoUpdaterStatus     \| Enable or disable the auto-updater (default: enabled)          \| disabled                                                               \|<br/>\| preferredNotifChannel \| Where you want to receive notifications (default: iterm2)      \| iterm2, iterm2\_with\_bell, terminal\_bell, or notifications\_disabled \|<br/>\| theme                 \| Color theme                                                    \| dark, light, light-daltonized, or dark-daltonized                      \|<br/>\| verbose               \| Whether to show full bash and command outputs (default: false) \| true                                                                   \|<br/><br/>Was this page helpful?<br/><br/>YesNo<br/><br/>Memory managementSecurity<br/><br/>xlinkedindiscord<br/><br/>On this page<br/><br/>* Settings files<br/>* Available settings<br/>* Settings precedence<br/>* Permissions<br/>* Tool-specific permission rules<br/>* Bash<br/>* Read & Edit<br/>* WebFetch<br/>* MCP<br/>* Auto-updater permission options<br/>* Recommended: Create a new user-writable npm prefix<br/>* Disabling the auto-updater<br/>* Optimize your terminal setup<br/>* Themes and appearance<br/>* Line breaks<br/>* Notification setup<br/>* Terminal bell notifications<br/>* iTerm 2 system notifications<br/>* Handling large inputs<br/>* Vim Mode<br/>* Environment variables<br/>* Configuration options<br/>* Global configuration |
